items.myth: Feature engineering encoding is the same topic as defining one-hot and ordinal encoding.
items.reality: Encoding Categoricals under data preprocessing owns the canonical definitions and pitfalls of one-hot, ordinal, and target encoding. This topic owns pipeline wiring, cardinality decisions, and when to invoke those methods—not redefining them.
items.example: A learner studies ordinal vs one-hot rules in preprocessing, then applies them inside a ColumnTransformer here without duplicating the nominal/ordinal table.

items.myth: Once you pick an encoder, cardinality no longer matters.
items.reality: Cardinality determines whether one-hot is feasible, whether bucketing is mandatory, and whether target or frequency compression is needed—the encoder choice follows the count of distinct training levels.
items.example: `country` with 12 levels fits one-hot; `device_id` with 200,000 levels forces a different branch in the same pipeline.

items.reality: A Pipeline stops train/test vocabulary leakage for one-hot, but naive target encoders inside the pipeline still leak unless they use out-of-fold statistics—see Encoding Categoricals for the safe pattern.
items.example: Wrapping `TargetEncoder` that computes global means in `fit` without CV still leaks labels; the pipeline shell does not fix the encoder logic.
